chunks.originalText:
He had now seen the full deformity of that creature
that shared with him some of the phenomena of consciousness, and was
co-heir with him to death: and beyond these links of community, which
in themselves made the most poignant part of his distress, he thought
of Hyde, for all his energy of life, as of something not only hellish
but inorganic. This was the shocking thing; that the slime of the pit
seemed to utter cries and voices; that the amorphous dust gesticulated
and sinned; that what was dead, and had no shape, should usurp the
offices of life. And this again, that that insurgent horror was knit to
him closer than a wife, closer than an eye; lay caged in his flesh,
where he heard it mutter and felt it struggle to be born; and at every
hour of weakness, and in the confidence of slumber, prevailed against
him, and deposed him out of life. The hatred of Hyde for Jekyll was of
a different order. His terror of the gallows drove him continually to
commit temporary suicide, and return to his subordinate station of a
part instead of a person; but he loathed the necessity, he loathed the
despondency into which Jekyll was now fallen, and he resented the
dislike with which he was himself regarded. Hence the ape-like tricks
that he would play me, scrawling in my own hand blasphemies on the
pages of my books, burning the letters and destroying the portrait of
my father; and indeed, had it not been for his fear of death, he would
long ago have ruined himself in order to involve me in the ruin. But
his love of life is wonderful; I go further: I, who sicken and freeze at
the mere thought of him, when I recall the abjection and passion of
this attachment, and when I know how he fears my power to cut him off
by suicide, I find it in my heart to pity him. It is useless, and the time awfully fails me, to prolong this
description; no one has ever suffered such torments, let that suffice;
and yet even to these, habit brought—no, not alleviation—but a certain
callousness of soul, a certain acquiescence of despair; and my
punishment might have gone on for years, but for the last calamity
which has now fallen, and which has finally severed me from my own face
and nature. My provision of the salt, which had never been renewed
since the date of the first experiment, began to run low. I sent out
for a fresh supply and mixed the draught; the ebullition followed, and
the first change of colour, not the second; I drank it and it was
without efficiency. You will learn from Poole how I have had London
ransacked; it was in vain; and I am now persuaded that my first supply
was impure, and that it was that unknown impurity which lent efficacy
to the draught. About a week has passed, and I am now finishing this statement under
the influence of the last of the old powders. This, then, is the last
time, short of a miracle, that Henry Jekyll can think his own thoughts
or see his own face (now how sadly altered!) in the glass. Nor must I
delay too long to bring my writing to an end; for if my narrative has
hitherto escaped destruction, it has been by a combination of great
prudence and great good luck.
chunks.modernizedText:
He had now fully recognized the monstrous nature of the creature that shared his consciousness and was destined to die with him. Beyond these shared experiences, which were the most distressing part of his ordeal, he thought of Hyde, despite his vitality, as something not just evil, but lifeless. This was the horrifying part; that the filth of the pit seemed to cry out and speak; that the formless dust acted out and sinned; that something dead and shapeless could take on the functions of life. And this too, that this rebellious horror was bound to him more closely than a wife, more closely than an eye; it was trapped in his flesh, where he heard it whisper and felt it struggle to be born; and in his moments of weakness, and during his sleep, it overpowered him, and pushed him out of life. 

Hyde's hatred for Jekyll was of a different kind. His fear of the gallows drove him to repeatedly kill himself temporarily, and return to his lesser role as a part rather than a person; but he hated this necessity, he hated the despair that Jekyll had now sunk into, and he resented the dislike with which he himself was viewed. Hence the cruel pranks he would play on me, writing blasphemies in my own handwriting on the pages of my books, burning my letters and destroying my father's portrait; and indeed, if it weren't for his fear of death, he would have destroyed himself long ago just to drag me down with him. But his will to live is incredible; I'll go further: I, who feel sick and cold at the mere thought of him, when I remember the depth and intensity of his attachment, and when I know how he fears my power to end him through suicide, I find it in my heart to feel sorry for him. 

It's pointless, and time is running out for me, to extend this description; no one has ever suffered such torments, let that be enough; and yet even these, habit brought—not relief—but a certain numbness of soul, a certain acceptance of despair; and my punishment might have continued for years, if not for the final disaster that has now occurred, which has finally separated me from my own face and nature. My supply of the salt, which hadn't been replenished since the first experiment, started to run low. I sent out for a new supply and mixed the potion; the bubbling followed, and the first change of color, not the second; I drank it and it was ineffective. You'll learn from Poole how I've had London searched; it was in vain; and I'm now convinced that my first supply was impure, and that it was this unknown impurity that gave the potion its power. 

About a week has passed, and I'm now finishing this statement under the influence of the last of the old powders. This, then, is the last time, barring a miracle, that Henry Jekyll can think his own thoughts or see his own face (now so sadly changed!) in the mirror. I mustn't delay too long to finish my writing; for if my story has so far avoided destruction, it's been due to a combination of great caution and great luck.

chunks.originalText:
Should the throes of change take me in
the act of writing it, Hyde will tear it in pieces; but if some time
shall have elapsed after I have laid it by, his wonderful selfishness
and circumscription to the moment will probably save it once again from
the action of his ape-like spite. And indeed the doom that is closing
on us both has already changed and crushed him. Half an hour from now,
when I shall again and forever reindue that hated personality, I know
how I shall sit shuddering and weeping in my chair, or continue, with
the most strained and fearstruck ecstasy of listening, to pace up and
down this room (my last earthly refuge) and give ear to every sound of
menace. Will Hyde die upon the scaffold? or will he find courage to
release himself at the last moment? God knows; I am careless; this is
my true hour of death, and what is to follow concerns another than
myself. Here then, as I lay down the pen and proceed to seal up my
confession, I bring the life of that unhappy Henry Jekyll to an end.
chunks.modernizedText:
If I'm in the middle of transforming while writing this, Hyde will rip it to shreds. But if some time has passed since I've put it aside, his incredible selfishness and short-sightedness will probably save it once again from his destructive rage. The doom that's closing in on both of us has already changed and crushed him. Half an hour from now, when I'll once again and forever become that hated personality, I know I'll sit in my chair, shuddering and crying, or continue to pace up and down this room—my last safe place on earth—listening in terrified anticipation to every threatening sound. Will Hyde die on the gallows? Or will he find the courage to end his life at the last moment? Only God knows; I don't care. This is my true moment of death, and what happens next is someone else's problem. So here, as I put down the pen and get ready to seal up my confession, I bring the life of the unfortunate Henry Jekyll to an end.
